# Transcrição: 13-Nathan and the Pledge

- **Fonte:** 13-Nathan and the Pledge
- **Duração:** 06:31
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right, guys, we're at the final storyteller and the final storytelling slam of 2017. Put your hands together and welcome Matthew Dix. Thank you. Thank you. The phone rings and when I pick it up, there's a man on the other end. He's angry. He's a parent. He's the father of one of my fifth graders, and he's pissed. And I know this guy. I taught his daughter a couple years ago. So he's not the kind of man who flies off the handle. What he's saying is real. And as I listen to what he tells me has happened to his son on this day, he doesn't realize that he's sort of opening up my heart and exposing a wound that I've been trying to bury for a very long time. I first learned about the Pledge of Allegiance, the truth of it, back in Boy Scout Camp. A counselor teaching citizenship merit badge explained to us that the words under God were placed into the pledge, like later than it was originally formulated. In 1954, the Knights of Columbus stuck God in in hopes of bringing religion to America. And as a 15-year-old boy and an atheist, I was pissed when I discovered this. And I was that kind of atheist that walks around and tells religious people how stupid they are and how their magic bullshit is just bullshit, and they should believe everything that I believe. And so it really hit me hard. And I asked the counselor, I said, isn't that like a violation of my first amendment? And he said, he's only like 18 or 19, but he said, well, you don't have to say the pledge. And I thought, you're right. I'm never saying the Pledge of Allegiance again. And so I have a chance to take my stand that night because at the dining hall, we say the Pledge of Allegiance before dinner. And so I decide I'm not going to say it. So the scout master comes to the front, he taps on the microphone, he says, please rise, and everyone rises except for me for like two seconds. I will tell myself later that I rose because all the penises were eye level when they all stood up and it made me unsettled. That wasn't really true. I stood because I was afraid not to stand. And that was strange for me, because I am a person who tries to aggressively do what other people don't do. I grew up just outside of Boston and I decided as a kid to be a New York Yankees fans, just to spite people that were around me. And you cannot imagine how hard it is to be in Boston and to be a New York Yankees fan. I was a kid who in middle school taught himself to read books upside down just to piss off teachers. Like I'm the special breed of asshole who will spend hours and hours of effort to like get 30 seconds of irritation with someone later on in life. In high school, I used to eat potatoes like apples just because I knew it like made people unsettled to be around me. And so I should be able to not pledge as a boy scout as a 15 year old, but I can't because I'm afraid that they're gonna think I don't love my country.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

And I do, I really, I love where I am, but I wanna say this thing that I believe and I can't. And I go through the rest of my high school pledging the allegiance and every time it makes me crazy. And so when you graduate from high school, you tend to not have to say the pledge anymore until you become an elementary school teacher, which I did in 1999. And now I have to pledge every damn day. And every time I pledge with these kids, I think about how I don't want to pledge, but I don't want anyone to think I'm not a patriot. And so I go through 15 years of pledging every day with kids until I meet Nathan. Nathan is a kid who was like me as a kid. He does not give a shit about what people think. My girls decide that Tuesdays are two, two Tuesdays. They will wear their two twos on Tuesdays. Three weeks in, Nathan starts wearing two twos for the rest of the year. And I know he got shit for it, but he did it the whole way through. And Nathan's the first person I've ever met who doesn't pledge the flag. He sits in the way I wish I could. And when I ask him on the first day, why are you sitting? He says, I'm not allowed to vote. So until they let me vote, I'm not gonna pledge allegiance to this country. Amazing. And so I watch Nathan sit all year long until the day his father calls me yelling at me. I was absent. I was at like professional development, changing my life, I'm sure, in some significant way. And while I was gone, the substitute teacher forced Nathan to stand up and pledge. And his father's angry and Nathan's angry and I am angry, because now I feel like I've let this kid down because I should have been sitting with him all along. And so I go to school the next day. First thing, I sit him down at my desk and I say, Nathan, listen, I'm sorry that happened to you. And I apologize to you on behalf of the teacher who's not here. And I thank him for not creating a problem, agreeing to pledge rather than disrupting the classroom. Like thank you for what you've done. And he listens and he's a good kid, he gets it. And then the intercom comes on and the principal says, please rise for the pledge. And I stand and Nathan sits as I expect. But as I stand, I look over Nathan's head and not a single kid in my class is standing for the pledge. And it's not because they're protesting the pledge. It's because they're protesting what happened to Nathan the day before, the indignity. And for the first time in my life, I sit back down and I don't pledge allegiance. And for the rest of that school year, none of us in that class pledge allegiance, which is really awkward sometimes, because a teacher or even the principal will be in the room when the pledge starts and they will stand and they will see the class. And me just sitting respectfully and waiting for it to be done. And I know the principal is thinking like, what the hell is going on in this classroom? And I know my colleagues are sometimes wondering like, what the hell is Matt doing to these kids? Like, why is he creating this band of rebels? But what I would tell them if they actually brought those words to me, I would say, I didn't do anything to these kids.

## [06:03 - 06:31]

It's what they did to me. They gave me the courage to do something that I wanted to do as a 15 year old boy and a 20 year old man and a 30 year old man. And it wasn't until I met Nathan Eusman at the age of 41 that I found the courage through Nathan to do the thing I wanted to do. Nathan taught me that sometimes you can stand up for your country by sitting down. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
